a: Azure is Microsoft's cloud platform: a huge set of computers in Microsoft's data centers that you rent over the internet. Instead of buying your own servers, you pay Azure to run your apps, store your data, and handle the hardware for you.
fs: Azure is rented computing from Microsoft's data centers, billed for what you use.
fj: Thinking Azure is one product, not a menu of hundreds of separate services you pick from.

q: What is a resource in Azure?
a: A resource is a single thing you create and manage in Azure, like a virtual machine, a database, or a storage account. Each piece of cloud you turn on is its own resource that you can configure, pay for, and delete on its own.
fs: A resource is one manageable Azure item, an individual instance of a service.
fj: Assuming a resource is the whole app, when really an app is usually many resources working together.

a: A resource group is a folder-like container that holds related resources together. You group resources that share a lifecycle so you can deploy, manage, and delete them as one unit, which keeps your account organized.
fs: A resource group is a logical container grouping resources by shared lifecycle and ownership.
fj: Thinking a resource group locks resources to one region, but a group can hold resources from many regions.

a: A subscription is the billing and boundary container that links your account to the resources you create, with its own bill and limits. Every resource you make lives inside one subscription, so it controls who pays and how much you can use.
fs: A subscription is the billing and quota boundary that every resource is created inside of.
fj: Confusing a subscription with a resource group; the subscription is the bigger billing layer above many groups.

a: A region is a geographic location, like East US or West Europe, where Microsoft has data centers running your resources. You pick a region close to your users so your app responds faster and stays compliant with local data rules.
fs: A region is the physical location whose data centers actually host your resources.
fj: Picking a random region; the wrong one adds latency and not every service exists in every region.
